name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
تَبَـٰرَكَ ٱلَّذِى بِيَدِهِ ٱلْمُلْكُ وَهُوَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (١)
```

```english
Blessed is He in whose hands is all sovereignty, and He has power over all things. (1)
```

```arabic
ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلْمَوْتَ وَٱلْحَيَوٰةَ لِيَبْلُوَكُمْ أَيُّكُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلًا ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْغَفُورُ (٢)
```

```english
He, who created death and life that He may test you [to see] which of you is best in conduct. And He is the All-mighty, the All-forgiving. (2)
```

```arabic
ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ سَبْعَ سَمَـٰوَٰتٍ طِبَاقًا ۖ مَّا تَرَىٰ فِى خَلْقِ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ مِن تَفَـٰوُتٍ ۖ فَٱرْجِعِ ٱلْبَصَرَ هَلْ تَرَىٰ مِن فُطُورٍ (٣)
```

```english
He created seven heavens in layers. You do not see any discordance in the creation of the All-beneficent. Look again! Do you see any flaw? (3)
```

```arabic
ثُمَّ ٱرْجِعِ ٱلْبَصَرَ كَرَّتَيْنِ يَنقَلِبْ إِلَيْكَ ٱلْبَصَرُ خَاسِئًا وَهُوَ حَسِيرٌ (٤)
```

```english
Look again, once more. Your look will return to you humbled and weary. (4)
```

```arabic
وَلَقَدْ زَيَّنَّا ٱلسَّمَآءَ ٱلدُّنْيَا بِمَصَـٰبِيحَ وَجَعَلْنَـٰهَا رُجُومًا لِّلشَّيَـٰطِينِ ۖ وَأَعْتَدْنَا لَهُمْ عَذَابَ ٱلسَّعِيرِ (٥)
```

```english
We have certainly adorned the lowest heaven with lamps, and made them [the means of pelting] missiles against the devils, and We have prepared for them a punishment of the Blaze. (5)
```

```arabic
وَلِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بِرَبِّهِمْ عَذَابُ جَهَنَّمَ ۖ وَبِئْسَ ٱلْمَصِيرُ (٦)
```

```english
For those who defy their Lord is the punishment of hell, and it is an evil destination. (6)
```

```arabic
إِذَآ أُلْقُوا۟ فِيهَا سَمِعُوا۟ لَهَا شَهِيقًا وَهِىَ تَفُورُ (٧)
```

```english
When they are thrown in it, they hear it blaring, as it seethes, (7)
```

```arabic
تَكَادُ تَمَيَّزُ مِنَ ٱلْغَيْظِ ۖ كُلَّمَآ أُلْقِىَ فِيهَا فَوْجٌ سَأَلَهُمْ خَزَنَتُهَآ أَلَمْ يَأْتِكُمْ نَذِيرٌ (٨)
```

```english
almost exploding with rage. Whenever a group is thrown in it, its keepers will ask them, ‘Did not any warner come to you?’ (8)
```

```arabic
قَالُوا۟ بَلَىٰ قَدْ جَآءَنَا نَذِيرٌ فَكَذَّبْنَا وَقُلْنَا مَا نَزَّلَ ٱللَّهُ مِن شَىْءٍ إِنْ أَنتُمْ إِلَّا فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ كَبِيرٍ (٩)
```

```english
They will say, ‘Yes, a warner did come to us, but we impugned [him] and said, ‘Allah did not send down anything; you are only in great error.’ (9)
```

```arabic
وَقَالُوا۟ لَوْ كُنَّا نَسْمَعُ أَوْ نَعْقِلُ مَا كُنَّا فِىٓ أَصْحَـٰبِ ٱلسَّعِيرِ (١٠)
```

```english
And they will say, ‘Had we listened or applied reason, we would not have been among inmates of the Blaze.’ (10)
```

```arabic
فَٱعْتَرَفُوا۟ بِذَنۢبِهِمْ فَسُحْقًا لِّأَصْحَـٰبِ ٱلسَّعِيرِ (١١)
```

```english
Thus they will admit their sin. So away with the inmates of the Blaze! (11)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَخْشَوْنَ رَبَّهُم بِٱلْغَيْبِ لَهُم مَّغْفِرَةٌ وَأَجْرٌ كَبِيرٌ (١٢)
```

```english
Indeed for those who fear their Lord in secret there will be forgiveness and a great reward. (12)
```

```arabic
وَأَسِرُّوا۟ قَوْلَكُمْ أَوِ ٱجْهَرُوا۟ بِهِۦٓ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ عَلِيمٌۢ بِذَاتِ ٱلصُّدُورِ (١٣)
```

```english
Speak secretly, or do so openly, indeed He knows well what is in the breasts. (13)
```

```arabic
أَلَا يَعْلَمُ مَنْ خَلَقَ وَهُوَ ٱللَّطِيفُ ٱلْخَبِيرُ (١٤)
```

```english
Would He who has created not know? And He is the All-attentive, the All-aware. (14)
```

## (Commentary)

The purpose of the Surah is to explain the universality of His Lordship (الربوبية) — *the absolute sovereignty and sustaining governance of Allah over all creation* — over all the worlds, in response to the claims of the idolaters who asserted that every part of the world has a lord from among the angels and others, and that He, the Exalted, is only the Lord of lords.

For this reason, He, the Glorified, enumerates many of His blessings in creation (الخلق) and governance (التدبير) — which serves as an argumentation for His Lordship — and opens the discourse with His being blessed (تباركه), which refers to the abundance of blessings (البركات) emanating from Him. He repeatedly describes Himself as the All-Beneficent (الرحمن) — *the possessor of expansive mercy that encompasses all things* — which is an intensive form of mercy (الرحمة) that signifies giving in response to the call of poverty. Within this is a warning that culminates in the mention of the Gathering (الحشر) and the Resurrection (البعث).

The contents of its verses can be summarized as a call to the monotheism of Lordship (توحيد الربوبية) and the affirmation of the Return (المعاد) — *the ultimate return of all beings to Allah for judgment*.

The Surah is Meccan, as evidenced by the context (سياق) of its verses.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **تَبارَكَ الَّذِي بِيَدِهِ الْمُلْكُ وَهُوَ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ** — **Blessed is He in whose hand is the kingdom, and He has power over all things.** (1)

The term **تبارك** (Blessed) regarding a thing means the abundance of goodness and blessings emanating from it.

His saying: **الَّذِي بِيَدِهِ الْمُلْكُ** (in whose hand is the kingdom) includes, by its absolute nature (إطلاقه), every form of sovereignty. Placing the kingdom "in His hand" is a metaphorical expression (استعارة بالكناية) for the perfection of His dominance over it and His disposal (تصرف) of it as He wills, just as a possessor of a hand disposes of what is in his hand and turns it as he wishes. Thus, He, the Exalted, possesses by His very essence everything from all its aspects, and He possesses whatever is possessed by anything else.

Therefore, describing Him, the Exalted, as the one "in whose hand is the kingdom" is broader than describing Him as the "Sovereign" (المليك) in His saying: **عِنْدَ مَلِيكٍ مُقْتَدِرٍ** — **in the presence of a puissant King** (54:55), and it is more explicit and emphatic than His description in: **لَهُ الْمُلْكُ** — **to Him belongs the kingdom** (64:1).

His saying: **وَهُوَ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ** (and He has power over all things) is an indication that His power (قدرته) is not limited by any boundary nor does it end at any limit. This is a necessary consequence of the absolute nature of the kingdom according to the context, even though the absolute nature of the kingdom — which is an attribute of action (صفات الفعل) — is among the necessary consequences of absolute power, which is an attribute of the essence (صفات الذات).

Furthermore, the verse contains an allusion to the proof for the possibility of the matter of the Return (المعاد) that is to follow.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **الَّذِي خَلَقَ الْمَوْتَ وَالْحَياةَ لِيَبْلُوَكُمْ أَيُّكُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلاً وَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْغَفُورُ** — **He, who created death and life that He may test you [to see] which of you is best in conduct. And He is the All-mighty, the All-forgiving.** (2)

**Life** (الحياة) is for a thing to be such that it perceives and wills, and **death** (الموت) is the absence of that. However, death, according to what appears from the teachings of the Quran, is a transition from one stage (نشأة) of the stages of life to another stage, as previously derived from His saying, the Exalted: **نَحْنُ قَدَّرْنا بَيْنَكُمُ الْمَوْتَ** (We have ordained death among you...) until His saying: **فِي ما لا تَعْلَمُونَ** (...in that which you do not know) (56:60-61). Thus, there is no obstacle to creation (الخلق) being associated with death just as it is with life.

Moreover, even if death were taken as a privative concept (عدمياً) as per common convention (العرف), it would be the privation of the faculty of life (عدم ملكة الحياة), and it possesses a share of existence (حظ من الوجود) that justifies the association of creation with it, similar to blindness in relation to sight and darkness in relation to light.

His saying: **لِيَبْلُوَكُمْ أَيُّكُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلاً** (that He may test you [to see] which of you is best in conduct) is the purpose (غاية) of His creation of death and life. **Testing** (البلاء) means examination (الامتحان). The meaning is that this type of creation — which is that you live and then die — is a preliminary, examinational creation by which those among you who are better in conduct are distinguished from others. It is well known that examination and distinction only occur for a matter that awaits you after this, which is the recompense (جزاء) of each according to their deed.

The discourse also contains an indication that the primary intended purpose (المقصود بالذات) of creation is the delivery of the good of the recompense, as He mentioned the excellence of conduct and the distinction of those who bring the best of it. Thus, those who are best in conduct are the intended ones of creation, and others are intended for their sake.

He concluded the discourse with His saying: **وَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْغَفُورُ** (And He is the All-mighty, the All-forgiving). He is the All-mighty (العزيز) because absolute kingdom and power belong to Him alone; no overcomer can overcome Him, and He has not empowered anyone to disobey Him except as a trial and examination, and He shall take vengeance upon them. He is the All-forgiving (الغفور) because He pardons many of their evil deeds in this world and will forgive many of them in the Hereafter as He has promised.

In concluding with these two names, there is also an element of instilling fear and hope (تخويف وتطميع) as required by the context of the call.

Know that the content of the verse is not merely a claim devoid of proof intended for indoctrination (تلقين), as might be imagined. Rather, it is a premise close to necessity (الضرورة) — or it is necessary — that demands the judgment of the necessity of the Resurrection for recompense. For a human being clothed in this worldly life followed by death is not free from acquiring the description of "best in conduct" or its opposite. He is equipped, according to the primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — with that which, were it not for the occurrence of an evil accident, would lead him to good conduct. Rarely is a human being free from acquiring one of the two descriptions, such as children and those in their position.

The acquired description resulting from the existence of a thing, which pervades most of its individuals, is a purpose (غاية) in its existence and intended in its creation. Just as the vegetative life of a certain tree, since it mostly leads to the production of a certain fruit, is considered the purpose of its existence and intended from it, likewise, righteousness and good conduct are the purpose of the creation of man. It is also known that righteousness and good conduct, if desired, are desired for something else, not for themselves. What is desired for its own sake is the "wholesome life" (الحياة الطيبة) that is not tainted by deficiency nor visited by vain talk or sinfulness. Thus, the verse is similar in meaning to His saying: **كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ذائِقَةُ الْمَوْتِ وَنَبْلُوكُمْ بِالشَّرِّ وَالْخَيْرِ فِتْنَةً** — **Every soul shall taste death, and We will test you with good and ill by way of a trial** (21:35).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **الَّذِي خَلَقَ سَبْعَ سَماواتٍ طِباقاً** — **He, who created seven heavens in layers.** (3) etc. That is, matching one above the other, or some resembling others — as has been conjectured. Some of what we can say regarding this has already passed in the commentary of Surah Ha Mim al-Sajdah.

His saying: **ما تَرى فِي خَلْقِ الرَّحْمنِ مِنْ تَفاوُتٍ** — **You do not see any discordance in the creation of the All-beneficent.** (3). Al-Raghib said: **Fawt** (الفوت) is the distance of a thing from a person such that its perception becomes impossible. The Exalted said: **وَإِنْ فاتَكُمْ شَيْءٌ مِنْ أَزْواجِكُمْ إِلَى الْكُفَّارِ** — **And if any of your wives should go over to the faithless** (60:11).

He said: **Tafawut** (التفاوت) is the difference in attributes, as if the attribute of one misses (يفوت) the other, or the attribute of each misses the other. The Exalted said: **ما تَرى فِي خَلْقِ الرَّحْمنِ مِنْ تَفاوُتٍ** meaning there is nothing in it that departs from the requirements of wisdom (الحكمة). [End of quote].

The intended meaning of denying discordance (التفاوت) is the continuity of governance (التدبير) and the interconnectedness of things with one another in terms of the ends (الغايات) and benefits resulting from their mutual interaction. The clashing of different causes in creation and their contention is like the struggle between the two pans of a scale, contending through weight and lightness, rising and falling; for while they differ, they agree in assisting the one who holds the scale in what he intends of determining the weight of the weighed commodity.

Thus, Allah has arranged the parts of creation such that they lead to their objectives without any part missing the purpose of another, or any part missing the attribute necessary for it to achieve the desired end.

The address in **ما تَرى** (You do not see) is a general address to everyone capable of seeing. In attributing creation to the All-Beneficent (الرحمن), there is an indication that the purpose of it is general mercy. The indefiniteness (تنكير) of **تَفاوُتٍ** (discordance) in the context of negation, and the introduction of the name "the All-Beneficent" over it, is to signify universality (العموم).

His saying: **فَارْجِعِ الْبَصَرَ هَلْ تَرى مِنْ فُطُورٍ** — **Look again! Do you see any flaw?** (3). **Futor** (الفطور) is disorder and weakness. The meaning of "returning the sight" (إرجاع البصر) is to look a second time, which is a metaphor for precision in looking and deep contemplation.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ثُمَّ ارْجِعِ الْبَصَرَ كَرَّتَيْنِ يَنْقَلِبْ إِلَيْكَ الْبَصَرُ خاسِئاً وَهُوَ حَسِيرٌ** — **Then look again and again: your look will return to you humbled and weary.** (4). **Al-Khasi'** (الخاسئ) is from the word for the eye being "driven back" (خسأ) when it withdraws out of humiliation, as Al-Raghib said. He also said: **Al-Hasir** (الحاسر) is the exhausted one due to the depletion of his powers. The exhausted one is called *hasir* and *mahsūr*. As for *hasir*, it is conceived that he himself has exhausted his power, and as for *mahsūr*, it is conceived that fatigue has exhausted him. The saying of the Mighty and Majestic: **يَنْقَلِبْ إِلَيْكَ الْبَصَرُ خاسِئاً وَهُوَ حَسِيرٌ** can correctly mean *hasir* or *mahsūr*. [End of quote].

His saying: **كَرَّتَيْنِ** (twice/again and again). **Al-Karrah** (الكرة) is the return, and the dual form here is intended to mean multiplicity and repetition. The meaning is: then return the sight return after return — i.e., many returns — and the sight will return to you withdrawn and humbled, while it is dull and exhausted, having found no flaw.

Thus, it is indicated in these two verses that the system running in the universe is a single system, continuous in its parts and interconnected in its dimensions.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلَقَدْ زَيَّنَّا السَّماءَ الدُّنْيا بِمَصابِيحَ** — **We have certainly adorned the lowest heaven with lamps** (5) until the end of the verse. **Masabih** (المصابيح) is the plural of *misbah*, which is a lamp. The stars are called lamps because of their illumination and brightness. Discussion on this has already passed in the commentary of Surah Ha Mim al-Sajdah.

His saying: **وَجَعَلْناها رُجُوماً لِلشَّياطِينِ** — **and made them missiles for the devils** (5). That is, We made the stars with which We adorned the heaven as missiles with which the devils who eavesdrop are pelted, as the Exalted said: **إِلَّا مَنِ اسْتَرَقَ السَّمْعَ فَأَتْبَعَهُ شِهابٌ مُبِينٌ** — **except him who eavesdrops, whereat a manifest flame pursues him** (15:18), and He said: **إِلَّا مَنْ خَطِفَ الْخَطْفَةَ فَأَتْبَعَهُ شِهابٌ ثاقِبٌ** — **except him who snatches a fragment, whereat a piercing flame pursues him** (37:10).

It is said: The sentence is evidence that what is meant by the stars with which the heaven is adorned is the totality of the original stars and the celestial meteors (الشهب), for the original stars do not move from their fixed positions. The terms "star" (الكوكب) and "heavenly body" (النجم) are applied to meteors just as they are applied to the original celestial bodies.

It is also said: Meteors separate from the stars to become missiles for the devils, while the stars themselves do not move unless Allah intends their annihilation.

This view is more in line with contemporary scientific perspectives. Some discussion has already passed regarding the meaning of pelting the devils with meteors.

His saying: **وَأَعْتَدْنا لَهُمْ عَذابَ السَّعِيرِ** — **and We have prepared for them the punishment of the Blaze.** (5). That is, We have prepared for the devils — who are the wicked among the Jinn — the punishment of the kindled, flaming fire.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِرَبِّهِمْ عَذابُ جَهَنَّمَ وَبِئْسَ الْمَصِيرُ** — **For those who defy their Lord is the punishment of hell, and it is an evil destination.** (6). When He presented some of the signs of His Lordship, He followed them with the threat against those who disbelieve in His Lordship, as is the characteristic of this Surah in the interweaving of proofs, threats, and warnings.

What is meant by "those who disbelieve in their Lord" is broader than the idolaters who deny His Lordship for any other than their "lords" and say that He, the Exalted, is only the Lord of lords; and those who deny it absolutely; and those who affirm His Lordship while differentiating between Him and His messengers, like the Jews and Christians who believed in some of His messengers and disbelieved in others.

The verse is also connected to His saying: **الَّذِي خَلَقَ الْمَوْتَ وَالْحَياةَ لِيَبْلُوَكُمْ أَيُّكُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلاً وَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْغَفُورُ** because of the indication it contains regarding the Resurrection and recompense, and it is connected to what preceded it as a generalization after a specification.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِذا أُلْقُوا فِيها سَمِعُوا لَها شَهِيقاً وَهِيَ تَفُورُ تَكادُ تَمَيَّزُ مِنَ الْغَيْظِ** — **When they are thrown into it, they hear it gasping as it seethes. It almost bursts with fury.** (7-8). Al-Raghib said: **Shahīq** (الشهيق) is the length of the inhalation (الزفير), which is the drawing in of breath, and **Zafīr** (الزفير) is its extension. [End of quote]. **Fawaran** (الفوران), as in *al-Majma'*, is the rising of boiling. **Tamayyuz** (التميز) is to break apart and separate. **Ghayz** (الغيظ) is the intensity of anger. The meaning is: when the disbelievers are cast into Hell, they hear it gasping — that is, it draws them into its interior just as air is drawn by inhalation into the chest — while it boils with them, raising and lowering them, almost disintegrating from the intensity of its anger.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **كُلَّما أُلْقِيَ فِيها فَوْجٌ سَأَلَهُمْ خَزَنَتُها أَلَمْ يَأْتِكُمْ نَذِيرٌ** — **Whenever a group is thrown into it, its keepers will ask them, ‘Did there not come to you any warner?’** (8). **Fawj** (الفوج) — as Al-Raghib said — is a group passing quickly. In His saying: **كُلَّما أُلْقِيَ فِيها فَوْجٌ** there is an indication that the disbelievers are cast into the Fire group by group, as indicated by His saying: **وَسِيقَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا إِلى جَهَنَّمَ زُمَراً** — **The faithless will be driven to hell in throngs** (39:71). They are cast in this manner because of the followers joining their leaders in misguidance, as the Exalted said: **وَيَجْعَلَ الْخَبِيثَ بَعْضَهُ عَلى بَعْضٍ فَيَرْكُمَهُ جَمِيعاً فَيَجْعَلَهُ فِي جَهَنَّمَ** — **and put the bad ones one on top of another, and heap them all together and put them into hell** (8:37). Some clarification of this has already passed under that verse in Surah al-Anfal.

**Khazanah** (الخزنة) is the plural of *khāzin*, who is the guardian over a stored thing. What is meant by them are the angels entrusted with the Fire, who manage the types of its punishment. The Exalted said: **عَلَيْها مَلائِكَةٌ غِلاظٌ شِدادٌ** — **over it are angels, stern and severe** (66:6), and He said: **وَما أَدْراكَ ما سَقَرُ** (And what will show you what is Saqar?) until He said: **عَلَيْها تِسْعَةَ عَشَرَ وَما جَعَلْنا أَصْحابَ النَّارِ إِلَّا مَلائِكَةً** — **Over it are nineteen. We have appointed only angels as keepers of the Fire** (74:27-31).

The meaning is: whenever a group from the groups of disbelievers driven to Hell is cast into it, the angels entrusted with the Fire and guarding it ask them — by way of rebuke — "Did there not come to you a warner?" meaning the warning Prophet.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالُوا بَلى قَدْ جاءَنا نَذِيرٌ فَكَذَّبْنا** — **They will say, ‘Yes, a warner did come to us, but we impugned [him]** (9) until the end of the verse. This is a narration of their answer to the question of the keepers, and in it is a confirmation that a warner did indeed come to them, whom they then attributed to falsehood, and an admission.

His saying: **ما نَزَّلَ اللهُ مِنْ شَيْءٍ** (Allah has not sent down anything) is an explanation of their denial, as is His saying: **إِنْ أَنْتُمْ إِلَّا فِي ضَلالٍ كَبِيرٍ** (You are only in great error). It is said: His saying: **إِنْ أَنْتُمْ** etc., is the speech of the angels addressing the disbelievers after their answer to the question; however, this is far-fetched from the context. Likewise is the possibility of it being from the speech of the messengers whom they denied, narrated by the angels to those disbelievers.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَقالُوا لَوْ كُنَّا نَسْمَعُ أَوْ نَعْقِلُ ما كُنَّا فِي أَصْحابِ السَّعِيرِ** — **And they will say, ‘Had we listened or applied reason, we would not have been among the inmates of the Blaze.’** (10). **Hearing** (السمع) is used to mean the perception of sound and speech by the organ, and sometimes it is used to mean its ultimate purpose among the rational, which is to commit to its requirements in action and omission. **Intellect** (العقل) is used for the discernment of good from evil and the beneficial from the harmful, and sometimes it is used to mean its ultimate purpose, which is to commit to its requirements in seeking good and benefit and avoiding evil and harm. The Exalted said: **لَهُمْ قُلُوبٌ لا يَفْقَهُونَ بِها وَلَهُمْ أَعْيُنٌ لا يُبْصِرُونَ بِها وَلَهُمْ آذانٌ لا يَسْمَعُونَ بِها أُولئِكَ كَالْأَنْعامِ بَلْ هُمْ أَضَلُّ** — **They have hearts with which they do not understand, they have eyes with which they do not see, and they have ears with which they do not hear. They are like cattle; rather they are more astray.** (7:179).

Most of those who benefit from hearing are the common people, due to their inability to intellectually grasp the subtleties of matters, perceive their reality, and be guided to their benefits and harms. Only the elite (الخاصة) benefit from the intellect.

Thus, in His saying: **لَوْ كُنَّا نَسْمَعُ أَوْ نَعْقِلُ** (Had we listened or applied reason), hearing is intended as responding to the call of the messengers and committing to the requirements of their speech, for they are the sincere and trustworthy advisors. By intellect is intended committing to the requirements of the truth they call to by intellectually grasping it and being intellectually guided to the fact that it is the truth and that it is mandatory for a human being to submit to the truth.

Hearing was mentioned before intellect because its use is the characteristic of the common people, who are the majority, while intellect is the characteristic of the elite, who are few individuals.

The meaning is: had we in the world obeyed the messengers in their advice and exhortations, or had we intellectually grasped the proof of the truth, we would not be today among the companions of the Blaze, who are the inhabitants of the Fire eternally.

It is said: Hearing and intellect were combined because the basis of religious obligation (التكليف) rests upon the proofs of hearing (revelation) and intellect.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَاعْتَرَفُوا بِذَنْبِهِمْ فَسُحْقاً لِأَصْحابِ السَّعِيرِ** — **Thus they will confess their sin. So away with the inmates of the Blaze!** (11). They only said: **لَوْ كُنَّا نَسْمَعُ أَوْ نَعْقِلُ ما كُنَّا فِي أَصْحابِ السَّعِيرِ** out of regret for what they neglected regarding the side of Allah and the good they missed for themselves. Thus, they confessed that what they committed resulted in entering the Fire and that they should not have committed it; this is the sin (الذنب), and so they confessed their sin.

The word "sin" (الذنب) was used in the singular based on the intention of its root meaning (المصدر), as it is originally a verbal noun.

His saying: **فَسُحْقاً لِأَصْحابِ السَّعِيرِ** (So away with the inmates of the Blaze!). **Suhq** (السحق) is the crushing of a thing into fragments, as mentioned by Al-Raghib, and it is a supplication against them.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَخْشَوْنَ رَبَّهُمْ بِالْغَيْبِ لَهُمْ مَغْفِرَةٌ وَأَجْرٌ كَبِيرٌ** — **Indeed those who fear their Lord in secret will have forgiveness and a great reward.** (12). When He mentioned the state of the disbelievers and what they are recompensed with for their disbelief, He contrasted it with the state of the believers in the Unseen (الغيب) — *that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge* — to complete the division. He mentioned "fear" (الخشية) as their attribute because the context is one of warning and threat.

He described their fear as being "in the Unseen" because that which they believe in is veiled from them behind the veils of the Unseen.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَأَسِرُّوا قَوْلَكُمْ أَوِ اجْهَرُوا بِهِ إِنَّهُ عَلِيمٌ بِذاتِ الصُّدُورِ** — **Whether you keep your discourse secret or make it public, He knows well what is in the breasts.** (13). This removes a doubt that might occur in their hearts based on a sense of improbability. This is because He, the Exalted, directed the discourse toward explaining His Lordship over everything, which entails the Resurrection and recompense, and He mentioned His absolute kingdom and power, and His creation and governance, but He did not mention His knowledge (علمه) which encompasses them and their states and deeds — and the Resurrection and recompense cannot be completed without it.

It was possible for them to imagine that deeds, in their vast number beyond counting, could not be recorded, especially what the breasts conceal. For man measures things by himself and weighs them by his own scale, and he is unable to count the particulars of deeds, which are various and passing movements, especially the deeds of the hearts hidden in their corners.

So He refuted this by stating that making speech public or hiding it is the same in relation to Him, the Exalted, for He is knowledgeable of what is within the breasts. The context bears witness that what is intended is the equality of the hidden and manifest aspects of deeds in relation to Him. He only mentioned the secrets of speech and its publicity because the meaning of concealment and manifestation is apparent in public and secret speech.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَلا يَعْلَمُ مَنْ خَلَقَ وَهُوَ اللَّطِيفُ الْخَبِيرُ** — **Would He not know those He has created, while He is the All-attentive, the All-aware?** (14). This is a rhetorical question (استفهام إنكاري) taken as a proof for His knowledge, the Exalted, of the deeds of creation, their manifest and hidden, their secret and public. This is because the deeds of creation — including the voluntary deeds of man — even if attributed to their agents, Allah the Glorified is the one who wills them and brings them into existence through the path of human choice and the requirement of other causes. He is the Creator of the essences of things and the one who ordains for them their effects, whatever they may be, and the link between them and their effects, bringing them to their effects. The Exalted said: **اللهُ خالِقُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ وَهُوَ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ وَكِيلٌ** — **Allah is the creator of all things, and He is the guardian of all things** (39:62), and He said: **الَّذِي خَلَقَ فَسَوَّى وَالَّذِي قَدَّرَ فَهَدى** — **who created and proportioned, who determined and guided** (87:2-3). Thus, He, the Glorified, encompasses the essence of what He created and its effect; and among its effects are its manifest and hidden deeds, what it conceals and what it makes public. How then could He encompass it and not know it?

In the verse is an indication that the states of things and their deeds are not outside of their creation, because He, the Exalted, argued from His knowledge of "whom He created" to His knowledge of the specifics of their states and deeds. Had the states and deeds been outside the existence of their subjects, the argumentation would not be complete.

Furthermore, states and deeds are among the requirements of their subjects, and that to which the existence of a thing is attributed, the effects of its existence are also attributed to it.

His saying: **وَهُوَ اللَّطِيفُ الْخَبِيرُ** (while He is the All-attentive, the All-aware). That is, the one who penetrates into the inner depths of things, informed of the particulars of their existence and their effects. The sentence is circumstantial (حالية), providing the reason for what preceded it. These two noble names are among the Beautiful Names (الأسماء الحسنى) with which the verse was concluded to emphasize its content.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of narration from Sufyan ibn 'Uyaynah, from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **لِيَبْلُوَكُمْ أَيُّكُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلاً** (that He may test you [to see] which of you is best in conduct). He said: **ليس يعني أكثركم عملا ولكن أصوبكم عملا ، وإنما الإصابة خشية الله والنية الصادقة والخشية.** — "It does not mean the one of you with the most deeds, but the one of you with the most correct deeds. Correctness is only the fear of Allah, the sincere intention, and godwariness (الخشية)."

Then he said: **الإبقاء على العمل حتى يخلص أشد من العمل. ألا والعمل الخالص ـ الذي لا تريد أن يحمدك عليه أحد إلا الله ، والنية أفضل من العمل ألا وإن النية هي العمل.** — "Maintaining the deed until it becomes sincere is harder than the deed itself. Behold, the sincere deed is that for which you do not want anyone to praise you except Allah. The intention is better than the deed; behold, the intention is the deed itself." Then he recited His saying: **قُلْ كُلٌّ يَعْمَلُ عَلى شاكِلَتِهِ** — **Say, ‘Everyone acts according to his character’** (17:84), meaning according to his intention.

In *al-Majma'*, Abu Qatadah said: "I asked the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) about His saying, the Exalted: **أَيُّكُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلاً** (which of you is best in conduct), what did He mean by it? He said: **يقول : أيكم أحسن عقلا.** — 'He says: which of you is best in intellect.' Then he said: **أتمكم عقلا وأشدكم لله خوفا ، وأحسنكم فيما أمر الله به ونهى عنه نظرا ـ وإن كان أقلكم تطوعا.** — 'The most complete of you in intellect, the most intense of you in fear of Allah, and the best of you in observing what Allah has commanded and forbidden — even if he is the least of you in supererogatory acts.'"

In the same source, from Ibn 'Umar, from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) that he recited His saying, the Exalted: **تَبارَكَ الَّذِي بِيَدِهِ الْمُلْكُ** until His saying **أَيُّكُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلاً**, then he said: **أيكم أحسن عقلا ، وأورع عن محارم الله وأسرع في طاعة الله.** — "Which of you is best in intellect, most wary of the prohibitions of Allah, and quickest in the obedience of Allah."

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*: regarding His saying, the Exalted: **الَّذِي خَلَقَ سَبْعَ سَماواتٍ طِباقاً** (He, who created seven heavens in layers). He said: **بعضها طبق لبعض.** — "Some of them are a layer for others."

In the same source: regarding His saying, the Exalted: **مِنْ تَفاوُتٍ** (any discordance). He said: **من فساد.** — "Any corruption."

In the same source: regarding His saying, the Exalted: **ثُمَّ ارْجِعِ الْبَصَرَ** (Then look again). He said: **انظر في ملكوت السماوات والأرض.** — "Look into the kingdom of the heavens and the earth."

In the same source: regarding His saying, the Exalted: **بِمَصابِيحَ** (with lamps). He said: **بالنجوم.** — "With the stars."

In the same source: regarding His saying, the Exalted: **سَمِعُوا لَها شَهِيقاً** (they hear it gasping). He said: **وقعا.** — "A thudding sound."

In the same source: regarding His saying, the Exalted: **تَكادُ تَمَيَّزُ مِنَ الْغَيْظِ** (It almost bursts with fury). He said: **على أعداء الله.** — "Against the enemies of Allah."

In the same source: regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَقالُوا لَوْ كُنَّا نَسْمَعُ أَوْ نَعْقِلُ ـ ما كُنَّا فِي أَصْحابِ السَّعِيرِ** (And they will say, ‘Had we listened or applied reason, we would not have been among the inmates of the Blaze’). He said: **قد سمعوا وعقلوا ولكنهم لم يطيعوا ولم يقبلوا ، والدليل على أنهم قد سمعوا وعقلوا ولم يقبلوا ، قوله : « فَاعْتَرَفُوا بِذَنْبِهِمْ فَسُحْقاً لِأَصْحابِ السَّعِيرِ » .** — "They had indeed heard and reasoned, but they did not obey and did not accept. The proof that they had heard and reasoned but did not accept is His saying: 'Thus they will confess their sin. So away with the inmates of the Blaze!'"

**I say:** He (peace be upon him) means that the evidence that the lack of hearing and intellect refers to the lack of obedience and acceptance after hearing and reasoning is that He, the Exalted, called their saying an "admission of sin" (اعترافا بالذنب). An act is not considered a sin on the part of its doer except after knowledge of the aspect of its evil through hearing or intellect.
